topic: A person's online friend turns out to live in the same city
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a cheerful little update from 'PixelDreamer101.' I scrolled through the usual curated snapshots—beach sunsets, artisanal coffee shops, perfectly filtered cityscapes. Then, the location tag caught my eye. It was downtown. My heart gave a slow, heavy thud against my ribs. I leaned back in my chair, a tight knot forming in my stomach. I’d spent weeks building this carefully constructed distance, this illusion of separate worlds. Now, the reality was collapsing. I opened a new chat window, my fingers hovering over the keyboard, a cold resentment starting to bubble up. "Oh, really?" I typed, my tone deliberately flat, masking the sudden, sharp clench in my jaw. I wanted to see the look on their face when they realized I knew.

[story 2]

I stared at the map, tracing the line between my apartment complex and the coordinates PixelDreamer101 had just shared. It was an absurd coincidence, a cruel twist of fate that felt entirely orchestrated. A slow burn started in my chest, a simmering heat that made my palms sweat. I didn't reply immediately. Instead, I went through my contacts, finding the number for the person who had been relentlessly mocking my recent posts. A small, almost imperceptible smirk touched my lips as I prepared the message. "Guess we share a zip code," I wrote, the words tasting metallic and sharp. I hit send before I could second-guess the venom in my tone. I needed to see how they reacted to this sudden, unwelcome intrusion into their carefully maintained facade.

[story 3]

The realization hit me like a physical blow when I saw the venue tag in their latest story—a specific gallery opening I’d been planning to attend next week. It was the same gallery. The same neighborhood. The air in my room suddenly felt thick, suffocating. I closed my laptop with a decisive snap, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. A wave of intense irritation washed over me, hot and demanding attention. I stood up, pacing the length of the living room, my movements jerky and agitated. I picked up my phone, my thumb hovering over the block of text. I didn't want an explanation; I just wanted to see the frustration bloom on their screen. I typed a single, pointed question, letting the sheer weight of my annoyance carry the message.
